attributes.stem:
A 51-year-old man suffered an acquired brain injury (ABI) and a fractured distal radius as a result of a fall in the setting of chronic alcoholism. He was managed with surgical intervention, commenced on enteral feeding and an indwelling catheter inserted whilst on the trauma ward. He has a medical history of hypertension and cardiac failure. His medications include perindopril and hydrochlorothiazide. He drinks 20 standard drinks per day. He has a poor diet consisting mainly of rice and fried chicken, according to his brother.

On review two days post op, he suddenly becomes unresponsive and not breathing. A shockable rhythm is identified and shocked delivered to the patient. He is successfully resuscitated and transferred to the Intensive Care Unit. The ECG performed immediately post-resuscitation demonstrated an increase in P wave amplitude, an increases in the PR interval, T wave inversion and an additional wave form between the T wave and consecutive P wave.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely cause of this presentation?
attributes.explanation:
In this case, the stem eludes to the patient having a poor diet and chronic alcoholism that increases the risk of deficiencies of vitamins, minerals, electrolytes as well as malnourishment. Hence, this patient is at risk of refeeding syndrome, a condition characterised by severe electrolyte abnormalities and fluid shifts witnessed during rehabilitation of malnourished patients with adequate nutritional intake, such as beginning patients on enteral feeding through a nasogastric tube. When energy in the form of glucose is available to malnourished patients, there is a sudden surge in insulin production which causes a shift in electrolytes from the extracellular to the intracellular space. One of the most dangerous electrolyte derangements that results from this electrolyte shift is hypokalaemia. When potassium levels are <2.5 mmol/L, there is significant risk of cardiac arrhythmias and even cardiac arrest.

Furthermore, the medication use (hydrochlorothiazide) is a potassium wasting diuretic that would no doubt contribute to his hypokalaemia. The ECG findings are hinting at the presence of hypokalaemia, particularly by the presence of an extra wave form (U wave).

For this patient to have arrested due to sepsis, they would likely have suffered from septic shock, which causes end organ hypoperfusion as a result of dysregulation of the normal response to infection. This patient has risk factors for the development of sepsis such as a hospital admission, recent surgery, alcohol misuse and an indwelling catheter in situ. It is unlikely that the patient would have developed sepsis in such a short time frame (two days post op) without treating clinicians noticing biochemical changes on routine bloods and he would no doubt have been placed on prophylactic antibiotics post op. The ECG is also indicative of hypokalaemia as the cause over a septic picture.

This patient has a history of cardiac failure which predisposes him to the development of acute decompensating cardiac failure, which is defined as a new or worsening onset of the signs of cardiac failure. If the decompensation is severe enough to cause an arrest then it is likely due to cardiogenic shock, which is a situation in which there is cardiac pump failure that results in a reduced CO. One of the causes of cardiogenic shock is cardiomyopathy, which in this case is likely to be dilated cardiomyopathy as a result of sustained alcohol misuse. If this was the cause of the arrest, the patient would present with tachypnoea, tachycardia, poor peripheral perfusion. Similarly, the ECG would present with features such as left ventricular stain in V5-V6, a left bundle branch block, axis deviation or even features of ventricular fibrillation, which are not seen in the above ECG.

This patient definitely has significant risk factors for the development of coronary artery thrombosis, including poor diet, central adiposity, hypertension and chronic congestive cardiac failure and alcoholism. However, the ECG post arrest did not elude to indicative changes of coronary artery thrombosis such as ST depression/elevation, or new bundle branch block.

A pulmonary embolism, or blood clot in the pulmonary vasculature is a definite cause of arrest in a patient should the embolism be large enough to cause significant strain on the heart. It would also likely present with a drop in oxygen saturations, chest pain as well as symptoms of a calf pain indicative of DVT, however given the patients condition this could not have been verbalised. It is unlikely that given only two minutes of CPR that the patient's arrest would have ceased without treatment with thrombolysis or anticoagulation.
learningPoints: Refeeding syndrome in severely malnourished patients may need to significant electrolyte abnormalities that are potentially life threatening.
Severe hypokalaemia is a risk factor for the development of cardiac arrest.
references: Gennari FJ. Hypokalemia. N Engl J Med. 1998;339(7):451-8. Available at: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/9700180/
Fuentebella J, Kerner JA. Refeeding syndrome. Pediatr Clin North Am. 2009;56(5):1201-10. Available at: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/19931071/
Mehanna HM, Moledina J, Travis J. Refeeding syndrome: what it is, and how to prevent and treat it. BMJ. 2008;336(7659):1495-8. Available at: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/18583681/
